reason:
Both answers converge on the same core recommendation: a short-term contractor to stabilize and execute a migration to managed Kubernetes, rather than rehiring a single full-time DevOps engineer who would recreate the bus-factor problem. Both correctly explain why self-managed k8s is disproportionate overhead for a 10-person team running 40 services, correctly compare managed control-plane cost to a DevOps salary, and correctly note the migration is low risk since workloads are already containerized. Neither explicitly raises the platform-simplification/consolidate-services point, so they are even there.

Minor differences: A ends with an explicit warning against re-hiring full-time DevOps unless growth justifies it, more directly guarding against recreating the single point of failure. B suggests hiring one platform-minded engineer after migration to own infra part-time, which drifts slightly back toward a single owner (softened by shared on-call), but B adds useful scope-discipline caveats (avoid full replatform to ECS/Cloud Run/Nomad, consider managed DB) that A lacks. These are small, roughly offsetting differences; both are correct, well-structured, and actionable.
